# Preface {.unnumbered}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

```{r}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| comment: NA

easypackages::packages(
  "bslib", 
  "cols4all", "covr", "cowplot", 
  "dendextend", "digest","DiagrammeR","dtwclust", "downlit", 
  "e1071", "exactextractr","elevatr", 
  "FNN", "future", "forestdata",
  "gdalcubes", "gdalUtilities", "geojsonsf", "geos", "ggplot2", "ggstats", 
  "ggspatial", "ggmap", "ggplotify", "ggpubr", "ggrepel", "giscoR", 
  "hdf5r", "httr", "httr2", "htmltools",
  "jsonlite", 
  "kohonen", 
  "leaflet.providers", "leafem", "libgeos","luz","lwgeom", "leaflet", "leafgl",
  "mapedit", "mapview", "maptiles", "methods", "mgcv", 
  "ncdf4", "nnet", 
  "openxlsx", "parallel", "plotly", 
  "randomForest", "rasterVis", "raster", "Rcpp", "RcppArmadillo", 
  "RcppCensSpatial","rayshader", "RcppEigen", "RcppParallel", 
  "RColorBrewer", "reactable", "rgl", "rsconnect","RStoolbox", "rts", 
  "s2", "sf", "scales", "sits","spdep", "stars", "stringr","supercells", 
  "terra", "testthat", "tidyverse", "tidyterra","tools", 
  "tmap", "tmaptools", "terrainr", 
  "xgboost",
  prompt = F)

#mapviewOptions(fgb = FALSE)
sf::sf_use_s2(use_s2 = FALSE)
```

```{r}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| comment: NA

options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org"),
        htmltools.dir.version = FALSE, 
        htmltools.preserve.raw = FALSE
        )

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE, 
  message = FALSE, 
  warning = FALSE,
  error = FALSE, 
  comment = NA, 
  tidy.opts = list(width.cutoff = 60)
  ) 
```

```{css, echo=FALSE, class.source = 'foldable'}
div.column {
    display: inline-block;
    vertical-align: top;
    width: 50%;
}

#TOC::before {
  content: "";
  display: block;
  height:200px;
  width: 200px;
  background-image: url('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seamusrobertmurphy/IPCC-wildfire-emissions/refs/heads/main/01-burn-area/AOI/aoi_burn_aa.png');
  background-size: contain;
  background-position: 50% 50%;
  padding-top: 80px !important;
  background-repeat: no-repeat;
}
```

This resource provides a demonstration of the IPCC Tier 1 method for estimating greenhouse gas emissions from biomass burning, implementing Equation 2.27 from the 2019 IPCC Refinement [@ipccChapter2Generic2019] end-to-end: satellite-based burned area detection, land cover stratification into IPCC fire categories, assignment of default fuel consumption values, and application of emission factors to derive CH~4~, N~2~O, and CO~2~ estimates formatted for UNFCCC reporting. All workflows use Honduras as the example AOI. To enable quick verification of values used, paged links to source materials are provided in footnotes throughout.

These examples also aim to address the core tension aroud fire emissions accounting and uncertainty: under the Managed Land Proxy (MLP), all fires on managed land, including natural wildfires, count toward a country's reported LULUCF balance. The 2019 Refinement (Section 2.6) added additional guidance to this optional disaggregation approach that separates natural disturbance spikes from background anthropogenic fire activity using statistical thresholds on long-term burned area distributions. This eBook implements both the standard Tier 1 calculation and the disaggregation framework, providing clear workflows in R and Python suitable for National Greenhouse Gas Inventory reporting and validation, structured around four chapters focusing on the following four main data components:

1.  [Burned Area](https://seamusrobertmurphy.quarto.pub/ipcc-fire-emissions/01-burn-area/): Delineate, extract and validate Tier 1 burned area spatial extents, apply uncertainty filters, aggregate temporally and handle missing data, compare against alternative fire data.
2.  [Fuel Stratification](https://seamusrobertmurphy.quarto.pub/ipcc-fire-emissions/02-fuel-stratification/): Classify burned pixels into Tier 1 fire types and fuel categories across forest, savanna, organic soils, using Tier 1 land cover, climate, and agroecological zones.
3.  [Fuel Consumption](https://seamusrobertmurphy.quarto.pub/ipcc-fire-emissions/03-fuel-consumption/): Apply Tier 1 default fuel consumption factors (M<sub>B</sub> × C<sub>f</sub> and compute fuel mass of different vegetation types according to season and management activity.
4.  [Emission Factors](https://seamusrobertmurphy.quarto.pub/ipcc-fire-emissions/04-emission-factors/): Derive Tier 1 emission factors (G) and compute biomass combustion of CH<sub>4</sub>, N<sub>2</sub>, and CO<sub>2</sub> gases and export to common reporting format.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Tier 1 Stratification Datasets[^index-1] {.unnumbered}

-   [MODIS MCD64A1 C6.1](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/MODIS_061_MCD64A1): Monthly burned area at 500 m resolution from 2000 to present sourced from Google Earth Engine Catalog (GEE) at `MODIS/061/MCD64A1`. Bands include `BurnDate`, `Uncertainty`, and `QA`.
-   [MODIS MCD12Q1 C6.1](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/MODIS_061_MCD12Q1): IGBP annual land cover (500 m). GEE: `MODIS/061/MCD12Q1`. Band: `LC_Type1`.
-   [MODIS MOD14A1 C6.1](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/MODIS_061_MOD14A1): Daily thermal anomalies / active fires (1 km). GEE: `MODIS/061/MOD14A1`. Band: `FireMask`.
-   [IPCC Global Climate Regions](https://philipaudebert.users.earthengine.app/view/ipcc-climate-zones) derived with IPCC 2006 [(& 2019](https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch03_Land%20Representation.pdf#page=47)) classification based on a) annual mean daily temperature, b) total annual precipitation, c) total annual potential evapo-transpiration, and d) elevation, and maintained by [ESDAC](https://esdac.jrc.ec.europa.eu/content/support-renewable-energy-directive#tabs-0-description=1) and individual [contributors](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.7303807).
-   [IPCC Global Soil Zones](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.catena.2024.108258) derived from the Harmonized World Soil Database (Caspari et al., 2022) and FAO SOTER, stratified into soil groups compiled by Batjes et al (2010; 2011) to assign Tier 1 SOC reference stocks per the IPCC 2019 Refinement classifications ([2026 Asset](https://code.earthengine.google.com/b5ecfdc0ed7543bb585b29a77e054915)).
-   [FAO Global Agro-Ecological Zones](https://data.apps.fao.org/gaez/): Global raster spatially classifying by agro-climatic zones based on climate, soil, and terrain. Used in IPCC Tier 1 fire emissions to stratify fuel consumption and aboveground emission factors, and derive soil reference stock strata by vegetation zone ([V2](https://data.apps.fao.org/catalog/iso/2fb209d0-fd34-4e5e-a3d8-a13c241eb61b), [V4](https://data.apps.fao.org/catalog/dataset/global-agro-ecological-zones-global-10km-gaez-v4), [V5](https://data.apps.fao.org/gaez/))
-   [FAO GAUL 2015](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/FAO_GAUL_2015_level1): First-level Administrative Unit Boundaries (`FAO/GAUL/2015/level1)`

## Validation Datasets

-   [Landsat 8 C2 T1 L2](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/LANDSAT_LC08_C02_T1_L2): Surface reflectance (30 m). GEE: `LANDSAT/LC08/C02/T1_L2`. Bands: `SR_B5` (NIR), `SR_B7` (SWIR2). Used for dNBR validation
-   [Hansen Global Forest Change v1.12:](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/UMD_hansen_global_forest_change_2024_v1_12) Tree canopy cover 2000 (30 m). GEE: `UMD/hansen/global_forest_change_2024_v1_12`. Band: `treecover2000`
-   [ESA WorldCover 2020 v100](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/ESA_WorldCover_v100_2020): Global land cover (10 m) published in Earth Engine Catalog asset at `ESA/WorldCover/v100/2020`.
-   [WorldClim V1 Bioclimatic Variables](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/WORLDCLIM_V1_BIO). 1 km resolution climatologies published in Earth Engine Catalog asset at `WORLDCLIM/V1/BIO`. Bands include `bio01` (mean annual temperature), `bio12` (annual precipitation) used for tropical/temperate/boreal delineation and humid tropical forest masking.
-   [USGS SRTM DEM](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/USGS_SRTMGL1_003): Shuttle Radar Topography Mission digital elevation model used in lowland organic soil filtering and zonal threshold checks, available 30m resolution asset in Earth Engine Catalog at `USGS/SRTMGL1_003`.
-   [FAOSTAT Emissions from Fires](https://www.fao.org/faostat/en/#data/GI): Global data desk processing CH₄, N₂O, and CO₂ emissions from biomass burning across vegetation types and organic soils, computed geospatially using IPCC 2006 Tier 1 default factors. Covers 1990–2024 by country, updated annually. Provides burned area (ha), burned biomass (tonnes), and emissions (kt) (FAO, [2025](https://files-faostat.fao.org/production/GI/GI_e.pdf)).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## IPCC Tier 1 Fire Emissions

The IPCC Tier 1 approach for estimating greenhouse gas emissions from fires provides a standardized, globally applicable framework based on readily available data and default emission factors. This methodology applies Equation 2.27[^index-2] from the 2019 IPCC Refinements to Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories [@ipccChapter2Generic2019]:

$$
L_{fire} = A \times M_B \times C_f \times G_{ef} \times 10^{-3}
$$

Where:

-   L~fire~ = greenhouse gas emissions from fire (tonnes of CH₄, N₂O, or CO₂)
-   A = area burnt (ha)
-   M~B~ = mass of fuel available for combustion (tonnes ha⁻¹)
-   C~f~ = combustion factor (dimensionless, proportion of fuel consumed)
-   G~ef~ = emission factor (g kg⁻¹ dry matter burnt)

#### Fire Types

-   Forest Fires: CH₄ and N₂O estimated via Equation 2.27 with G~ef~ from Table 2.5. CO₂ is accounted through carbon stock changes (Eq. 2.7–2.14), not the fire emissions table, because fire CO₂ and regrowth are not synchronous for woody biomass (2006 GL, s4.2.4) [@ipccChapter4Forest2006, pp. 4.27].[^index-3] Applies to IGBP classes 1–5, disaggregated into Humid Tropical Forest and Other Forest by climate zone overlay.
-   Savanna Fires: Non-CO₂ emissions only (CH₄, N₂O), assuming CO₂ balance through annual vegetation regrowth. Covers IGBP classes 6–10 (Shrublands, Woody Savannas, Savannas, Grasslands).
-   Organic Soil Fires: Reports both CO₂ and CH₄ using IPCC Wetlands Supplement parameters [@ipcc2013Supplement20062014]. Currently validated for Southeast Asia only; FAOSTAT sets emissions to zero outside Indonesia, Malaysia, and Brunei Darussalam.

-   CH₄ and N₂O estimated via Equation 2.27 with G~ef~ from Table 2.5
-   CO₂ accounted for through carbon stock changes (Eq. 2.7--2.14), not the fire emissions table, because fire CO₂ and regrowth are not synchronous for woody biomass (2006 GL, §4.2.4)
-   Applies to fires in Evergreen, Deciduous, and Mixed Forest land cover types

Savanna Fires:

-   Non-CO₂ emissions only (CH₄, N₂O)
-   Assumes CO₂ balance through annual vegetation regrowth
-   Covers Grassland, Savanna, Woody Savanna, and Shrubland types

Organic Soil Fires:

-   Reports both CO₂ and CH₄ emissions
-   Uses IPCC Wetlands Supplement parameters [@ipcc2013Supplement20062014]
-   Currently validated for Southeast Asia only

#### Managed Land Proxy Approach

The methodology adopts the Managed Land Proxy (MLP) as the primary framework for attributing anthropogenic emissions. Under MLP:

-   All emissions and removals on managed lands approximate anthropogenic effects
-   Natural disturbances (e.g., wildfires) are included in total managed land fluxes
-   Optional disaggregation separates anthropogenic activities from natural disturbance impacts
-   Expectation that emissions from natural disturbances balance with subsequent removals over time

The 2019 IPCC Refinement provides additional guidance to addressing inter-annual variability (IAV, [@ipccChapter2Generic2019, [pp 2.67](https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=67)]) using the Managed Land Proxy approach, specifically by disggregating between:

1.  Natural disturbances wildfires, insects, windthrow, ice storms
2.  Climate variability affecting photosynthesis and respiration
3.  Variation in human activities (harvesting, land-use change)

Countries experiencing high IAV from natural disturbances may optionally disaggregate these fluxes to better identify trends in anthropogenic emissions and evaluate mitigation actions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

```{r}
#| label: preface-gee-setup
#| include: false

# Point environment to your configured python
reticulate::use_python("/opt/local/bin/python", required = TRUE)

# Initialise Earth Engine
ee <- reticulate::import("ee")
ee$Initialize(project = "murphys-deforisk")
cat("GEE initialised\n")

# Helper: visualize EE tile in tmap/leaflet
ee_tile_url <- function(ee_image, vis_params) {
  ee_image$getMapId(vis_params)$tile_fetcher$url_format
}

# Helper: EE FeatureCollection => sf
ee_to_sf <- function(fc) {
  geojsonsf::geojson_sf(
    jsonlite::toJSON(fc$getInfo(), auto_unbox = TRUE))
}

# Load AOI boundaries from FAO GAUL
aoi_country_ee <- ee$FeatureCollection("FAO/GAUL/2015/level0")$
  filter(ee$Filter$eq("ADM0_NAME", "Honduras"))
aoi_states_ee  <- ee$FeatureCollection("FAO/GAUL/2015/level1")$
  filter(ee$Filter$eq("ADM0_NAME", "Honduras"))
aoi_country_sf <- ee_to_sf(aoi_country_ee)
aoi_states_sf  <- ee_to_sf(aoi_states_ee)
```

## FAOSTAT Fires Domain

The FAOSTAT Emissions from Fires domain provides spatially- and temporally stratified estimates of CH₄, N₂O, and CO₂ emissions from biomass burning across all countries and territories, covering 1990–2024 with annual updates [@eurostatGeographicInformationSystem2025]. FAOSTAT implements IPCC Tier 1 methodology via pixel-level processing in Google Earth Engine, country-level aggregation via FAO GAUL boundaries, and climate-dependent fuel consumption values stratified by FAO-FRA Ecological Zones. The database serves as an official IPCC-recommended tool for National Greenhouse Gas Inventory (NGHGI) QA/QC processes.

The sections below introduce each stratification dataset that feeds into Equation 2.27, visualized over the Honduras AOI used throughout this eBook.

### Activity Data: Burned Area (A)

MODIS MCD64A1 Collection 6.1 provides monthly burned area at 500 m resolution from 2001–present. FAOSTAT retains only pixels with uncertainty \< 20% and aggregates pixel-level detections to annual country totals. Historical coverage (1990–2000) is backfilled using earlier FAOSTAT estimates (1996–2000) and reconstructed averages (1990–1995) [@rossi2016faostat].

```{r}
#| label: preface-burned-area-map
#| code-summary: "MCD64A1 burned area composite (2000–2025)"

# Load MCD64A1 & filter to AOI & TOI
mcd64 <- ee$ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MCD64A1")
burned_area <- mcd64$
  filterBounds(aoi_country_ee$geometry())$
  filterDate("2000-01-01", "2025-12-31")

# 25-year composite: every pixel that ever burned
all_years_burn <- burned_area$
  select("BurnDate")$max()$gt(0L)$selfMask()$
  clip(aoi_country_ee$geometry())

tmap::tmap_mode("view")
tmap::tm_shape(aoi_states_sf) + tmap::tm_borders(col = "MAP_COLORS", lwd = 1) +
  tmap::tm_shape(aoi_country_sf) + tmap::tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 1.5) +
  tmap::tm_tiles(ee_tile_url(all_years_burn, list(palette = "red", min = 0L, max = 1L))) +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(type = "fill", col = "red", labels = "Burn Area",
                      title = "MCD64A1 Burn Areas (2000\u20132025)") +
  tmap::tm_scalebar(position = c("RIGHT", "BOTTOM"), text.size = 0.5) +
  tmap::tm_basemap("Esri.WorldImagery")
```

### Land Cover Stratification: Three Fire Categories

FAOSTAT reclassifies the MODIS MCD12Q1 IGBP 17-class land cover into three fire categories for Equation 2.27:

1.  Forest Fires — IGBP classes 1–5 (Evergreen Needleleaf, Evergreen Broadleaf, Deciduous Needleleaf, Deciduous Broadleaf, Mixed Forests). Disaggregated into Humid Tropical Forest and Other Forest based on climate zone overlay.
2.  Savanna Fires — IGBP classes 6–10 (Closed Shrublands, Open Shrublands, Woody Savannas, Savannas, Grasslands).
3.  Fires in Organic Soils — Identified independently via HWSD Histosol overlay, regardless of land cover class.

```{r}
#| label: preface-igbp-map
#| code-summary: "IGBP land cover classification (2020)"

# Load IGBP land cover for 2020
landCover <- ee$ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MCD12Q1")
lc_2020 <- landCover$
  select("LC_Type1")$
  filterDate("2020-01-01", "2020-12-31")$
  first()$
  clip(aoi_country_ee$geometry())

# IGBP 17-class palette
lc_vis <- list(
  min = 0L,
  max = 17L,
  palette = c(
    '05450a', '086a10', '54a708', '78d203', '009900',
    'c6b044', 'dcd159', 'dade48', 'fbff13', 'b6ff05',
    '27ff87', 'c24f44', 'a5a5a5', 'ff6d4c', '69fff8',
    'f9ffa4', '1c0dff'
  )
)

tmap::tmap_mode("view")
tmap::tm_shape(aoi_country_sf) + tmap::tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 1.5) +
  tmap::tm_tiles(ee_tile_url(lc_2020, lc_vis), group = "IGBP Land Cover 2020") +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(
    type = "polygons",
    fill = c("#05450a", "#086a10", "#54a708", "#78d203", "#009900",
             "#c6b044", "#dcd159", "#dade48", "#fbff13", "#b6ff05",
             "#27ff87", "#c24f44", "#a5a5a5", "#ff6d4c", "#69fff8",
             "#f9ffa4", "#1c0dff"),
    labels = c("1 Evergreen Needleleaf", "2 Evergreen Broadleaf",
               "3 Deciduous Needleleaf", "4 Deciduous Broadleaf",
               "5 Mixed Forests", "6 Closed Shrublands",
               "7 Open Shrublands", "8 Woody Savannas",
               "9 Savannas", "10 Grasslands",
               "11 Permanent Wetlands", "12 Croplands",
               "13 Urban", "14 Cropland/Veg Mosaic",
               "15 Snow/Ice", "16 Barren", "17 Unclassified"),
    title = "IGBP Land Cover (2020)") +
  tmap::tm_scalebar(position = c("RIGHT", "BOTTOM"), text.size = 0.5) +
  tmap::tm_basemap("Esri.WorldImagery")
```

#### Humid Tropical vs. Other Forest

Forest pixels are further disaggregated using a WorldClim-based climate proxy: pixels with mean annual temperature \> 18°C and annual precipitation \> 1,500 mm are classified as Humid Tropical Forest; all remaining forest pixels are classified as Other Forest. This determines which row of IPCC Table 2.4 supplies the fuel consumption value.

```{r}
#| label: preface-forest-type-map
#| code-summary: "Forest type split: Humid Tropical vs. Other Forest"

# Forest mask from IGBP classes 1-5
forest_mask <- lc_2020$gte(1L)$And(lc_2020$lte(5L))

# WorldClim climate proxy for tropical moist zones
bio <- ee$Image("WORLDCLIM/V1/BIO")
precip <- bio$select("bio12")  # Annual precipitation (mm)
temp   <- bio$select("bio01")  # Annual mean temp (°C * 10)

tropical <- temp$gt(180L)
moist    <- precip$gt(1500L)
humid_tropical_mask <- tropical$And(moist)

# Combine with forest mask
humid_tropical_forest <- forest_mask$And(humid_tropical_mask)
other_forest          <- forest_mask$And(humid_tropical_mask$Not())

tmap::tmap_mode("view")
tmap::tm_shape(aoi_country_sf) + tmap::tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 1.5) +
  tmap::tm_tiles(
    ee_tile_url(
      humid_tropical_forest$selfMask()$clip(aoi_country_ee$geometry()),
      list(palette = "darkgreen", min = 1L, max = 1L)
    ), group = "Humid Tropical Forest"
  ) +
  tmap::tm_tiles(
    ee_tile_url(
      other_forest$selfMask()$clip(aoi_country_ee$geometry()),
      list(palette = "orange", min = 1L, max = 1L)
    ), group = "Other Forest"
  ) +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(
    type = "polygons",
    fill = c("darkgreen", "orange"),
    labels = c("Humid Tropical Forest", "Other Forest"),
    title = "Forest Type Classification"
  ) +
  tmap::tm_scalebar(position = c("RIGHT", "BOTTOM"), text.size = 0.5) +
  tmap::tm_basemap("Esri.WorldImagery")
```

#### Organic Soil Screening

Fires in organic soils are identified by overlaying burned area with the Histosols class from the Harmonized World Soil Database (HWSD v1.2). The IPCC 7-class soil classification is derived from the HWSD via taxotransfer procedures developed by [@batjes2009ipcc; @batjes2010ipcc], which map FAO soil taxonomic units to the generalized classes (HAC, LAC, SAN, POD, VOL, WET, ORG) required for Tier 1 inventories. Due to high uncertainty and limited validation outside Southeast Asia, FAOSTAT sets organic soil fire emissions to zero for all countries except Indonesia, Malaysia, and Brunei Darussalam. Honduras has zero organic soil fire emissions in the FAOSTAT database.

::: {.callout-note title="Soil Classification Uncertainty for South America"}
The Batjes soil profile database underlying the IPCC defaults has known geographic biases relevant to fire emissions reporting in South America:

-   Brazilian data dominance: Warm-temperate moist (W1) SOC reference values are elevated because Brazilian profiles are overrepresented in the global dataset, resulting in 10–30% overestimation compared to median-based statistics [@batjes2010ipcc]. This directly affects fuel load assumptions when organic soil fire factors are applied in these zones.
-   Volcanic soil gaps: The IPCC VOL soil class is poorly represented in the global profile database. Andean volcanic soils (Andisols) have unique carbon stabilization properties (allophane complexation) that differ from global defaults. When páramo or high-altitude grasslands on volcanic substrates burn, applying standard fuel consumption values introduces unquantified bias.
-   Resolution limitations: At \~1 km resolution, the HWSD cannot resolve transitional landscapes such as Amazonian várzea floodplains, Andean wetlands, or mosaic peatland-mineral soil boundaries common in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. Mixed pixels at these transitions may systematically misclassify organic soil extent.
-   Depth limitation: SOC estimates are limited to the IPCC reference depth of 0–30 cm, yet tropical peat fires commonly burn to 30–50 cm depth. As [@batjes2011soc] noted, the relationship between SOC~30~ stocks and deeper carbon varies markedly by climate zone and soil class, meaning Tier 1 defaults may underestimate fuel available for deep peat combustion.
:::

### Fuel Consumption and Ecological Zones (M~B~ × C~f~)

The fuel consumption term M~B~ × C~f~ represents the mass of fuel actually consumed per hectare. FAOSTAT assigns these values from IPCC Table 2.4 (2019 Refinement, Vol. 4, Ch. 2), stratified by fire category and climate zone. Climate zone assignment uses FAO Global Ecological Zones for forest fires and JRC IPCC Climate Zones for savanna and organic soil fires [@fao2012global; @ipccChapter3Consistent2006].

| Fire Category | Vegetation Type | M~B~ × C~f~ (t DM ha⁻¹) | SE |
|----|----|----|----|
| Humid Tropical Forest | Tropical moist broadleaf | 163.6 | ±56.2 |
| Other Forest (Temperate) | Temperate broadleaf/mixed | 59.5 | ±24.8 |
| Other Forest (Boreal) | Boreal conifer | 40.0 | ±16.0 |
| Savanna (Tropical) | Tropical savanna/grassland | 6.2 | ±2.4 |
| Savanna (Temperate) | Temperate grassland | 4.1 | ±1.6 |
| Organic Soil (Tropical) | Tropical peatland | 353.0 | ±212.0 |

: IPCC Table 2.4 — Fuel consumption defaults (selected rows) {.striped .hover}

### Emission Factors (G~ef~)

Emission factors from IPCC Table 2.5 (2019 Refinement) convert fuel consumed to gas-specific emissions. Forest fires report only CH₄ and N₂O via Equation 2.27 (CO₂ is captured in carbon stock changes); savanna fires report CH₄ and N₂O; organic soil fires report CO₂ and CH₄.

| Fire Category           | CO~2~ (g kg⁻¹) | CH~4~ (g kg⁻¹) | N~2~O (g kg⁻¹) |
|-------------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Tropical Forest         | 1580           | 6.8            | 0.20           |
| Temperate Forest        | 1569           | 4.7            | 0.26           |
| Boreal Forest           | 1569           | 4.7            | 0.26           |
| Savanna/Grassland       | 1613           | 2.3            | 0.21           |
| Organic Soil (Tropical) | 1703           | 9.7            | —              |

: IPCC Table 2.5 — Emission factors (selected rows) {.striped .hover}

For UNFCCC reporting, CH₄ and N₂O are converted to CO₂-equivalents using AR5 GWP-100 values: CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265.

### QA/QC and Validation

The IPCC recommends FAOSTAT as a QA/QC tool for National GHG Inventories. Countries can cross-validate satellite-derived burned areas against national fire databases, test sensitivity to country-specific vs. default parameters, and identify data gaps or inconsistencies. Systematic differences between FAOSTAT (satellite-based, top-down) and NGHGI (ground-based, bottom-up) may indicate detection limitations, incomplete ground reporting, or biomass consumption parameter uncertainties.[^index-4]

Countries may use FAOSTAT as a stepping stone to higher-tier methods: Tier 2 replaces default emission factors with country-specific values and refines fuel consumption via field measurements; Tier 3 integrates process-based models (e.g., FOFEM, CONSUME) and measurement-based carbon stock assessments.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Uncertainty Considerations

Each term in the IPCC emission equation introduces uncertainty that compounds across the calculation chain. At Tier 1, cumulative uncertainty is typically ±30% for burned area, ±30–40% for CO₂ emissions, and ±50% for CH₄ and N₂O.

Activity data (burned area): MCD64A1 retains only pixels with uncertainty \< 20%, but global burned area may still be underestimated by 20–30% due to small fires missed at 500m resolution, canopy obscuration, cloud contamination, and rapid regrowth masking burn scars [@giglio2010assessing].

Land classification: MCD12Q1 achieves \~75% global accuracy, with confusion between woody savannas and forests directly affecting which fuel consumption values are assigned (Table 2.4 ranges: 2.6–163.6 t ha⁻¹).

Fuel consumption and combustion factors: IPCC defaults (Tables 2.4, 2.6) carry standard errors of 30–50% of mean values, reflecting natural variability across vegetation types, fire intensity, and fuel moisture conditions.

Emission factors: IPCC defaults (Table 2.5) based on [@andreae2001emission] represent global averages with standard deviations of 20–60%, influenced by the balance between flaming and smoldering combustion.

Organic soil fires carry particularly high uncertainty due to detection challenges, limited field validation outside Southeast Asia, and difficulty distinguishing surface from subsurface peat combustion.

Upstream soil classification bias: The determination of whether a burned pixel is classified as organic soil---and therefore whether peat fire emission factors are applied---depends on the IPCC soil class map derived from the Harmonized World Soil Database [@batjes2009ipcc; @batjes2010ipcc]. This classification introduces systematic uncertainties that propagate into fire emission estimates. The underlying SOC reference values (Table 2.3) are based on a global compilation of \>10,000 soil profiles [@batjes2011soc], but the dataset is geographically uneven: warm-temperate moist zones are dominated by Brazilian profiles, inflating SOC_REF values by 10–30% relative to median statistics. For South American fire reporting, this bias compounds with HWSD resolution limitations (\~1 km) that cannot resolve mosaic peatland-mineral soil boundaries in Amazonian floodplains and Andean wetlands, and with a 0–30 cm depth constraint that underestimates fuel available for deep tropical peat combustion. Volcanic soils (Andisols), widespread in Andean countries, are particularly underrepresented in the global profile database, leaving fuel consumption estimates for fires in páramo and high-altitude grasslands on volcanic substrates largely unvalidated.

#### Uncertainty Reduction and Inter-Annual Variability

Countries can reduce uncertainty by integrating national fire databases with satellite data, conducting field campaigns for fuel loads and combustion factors, developing regional emission factors, and implementing Tier 2/3 methods. Methodological enhancements include multi-sensor fusion (MODIS + VIIRS + Sentinel), sub-pixel burned area algorithms, and burn severity mapping.

High inter-annual variability (IAV) from natural disturbances---which can cause 2–3 orders of magnitude variation---complicates trend analysis and policy evaluation. Countries may optionally disaggregate natural disturbance emissions (beyond normal variability or 95% CI) from anthropogenic managed-land fluxes, improving clarity while maintaining MLP totals for official reporting [@ipccChapter2Generic2019][^index-5].

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Data Sources

#### MCD64A1.061: Burn Areas

Product Overview: The MCD64A1 Version 6.1 Burned Area product provides monthly, global gridded burned area at 500m spatial resolution, derived from combined Terra and Aqua MODIS observations.

Key Features:

-   Temporal Resolution: Monthly composites, 2001–present
-   Spatial Resolution: 500m nominal pixel size
-   Algorithm: Decision tree classifier using surface reflectance time series
-   Output: Burn date encoded as ordinal day of year, with quality indicators

Data Layers:

-   Burn Date (day of year when burn detected)
-   Burn Data Uncertainty (percentage uncertainty, 0-100%)
-   Quality Assurance flags

FAOSTAT Implementation:

-   Quality filter applied: Only pixels with uncertainty \< 20% are retained
-   Aggregation: Pixel-level burn dates accumulated to annual country totals
-   GEE Asset: `MODIS/061/MCD64A1`

Access:

-   NASA LP DAAC: https://lpdaac.usgs.gov/products/mcd64a1v061/
-   Google Earth Engine: `ee.ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MCD64A1")`
-   User Guide: [@giglio2024collection]

Limitations:

-   May miss small fires (\< 500m²)
-   Reduced accuracy in densely forested areas
-   Cloud obscuration limits temporal coverage in some regions
-   Detection lag in slow-spreading fires

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### MCD12Q1.061: IGBP Land Cover

Product Overview: MCD12Q1 provides annual global land cover classification at 500m resolution using supervised classification of MODIS reflectance data.

Classification Scheme: FAOSTAT uses Land Cover Type 1 (IGBP 17-class legend):

Forest Types (used for Forest Fire category):

-   Class 1: Evergreen Needleleaf Forests
-   Class 2: Evergreen Broadleaf Forests
-   Class 3: Deciduous Needleleaf Forests
-   Class 4: Deciduous Broadleaf Forests
-   Class 5: Mixed Forests

Grassland/Savanna Types (used for Savanna Fire category):

-   Class 6: Closed Shrublands
-   Class 7: Open Shrublands - Class 8: Woody Savannas
-   Class 9: Savannas
-   Class 10: Grasslands
-   Other Classes: Croplands, Urban, Wetlands, etc. (Classes 11-17)

FAOSTAT Implementation:

-   Annual land cover maps matched to fire detection year
-   Overlay burned pixels with contemporaneous land cover
-   Exclude built-up, barren, and water bodies
-   GEE Asset: `MODIS/061/MCD12Q1`

Access:

-   NASA LP DAAC: https://lpdaac.usgs.gov/products/mcd12q1v061/
-   Google Earth Engine: `ee.ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MCD12Q1")`
-   User Guide: [@sulla2018user]

Accuracy:

-   Overall accuracy: \~75% globally
-   Higher accuracy in homogeneous landscapes
-   sConfusion common between woody savannas and open forests

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### FAO Global Ecological Zones

Dataset Overview:\
The Global Ecological Zones (GEZ) classification provides a climate-vegetation framework for stratifying fuel consumption parameters.

Classification System Combines:

-   Climate domains (Polar, Boreal, Temperate, Subtropical, Tropical)
-   Ecological zones (e.g., Tropical moist forest, Tropical dry forest, Subtropical steppe)

FAOSTAT Implementation:

-   Overlay burned forest pixels with GEZ to distinguish:
-   Humid Tropical Forest: Tropical moist ecological zones
-   Other Forest: All remaining forest types
-   Assign climate-dependent fuel consumption values from IPCC Table 2.4

Access:

-   FAO Forestry: http://www.fao.org/forest-resources-assessment/
-   Document: FAO Global Ecological Zones for FAO forest reporting 2010 Update [@fao2012global]

Application:

-   Determines fuel biomass consumption parameters
-   Stratifies emission factors where climate-sensitive
-   Links to FAO Forest Resources Assessment (FRA) reporting categories

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### IPCC Soil Zones Layer

Dataset Overview:\
The Harmonized World Soil Database (HWSD v1.2) provides global soil type classifications at 30 arc-second (\~1 km) resolution.

Histosols Class:\
Organic soils characterized by:

-   High organic matter content (\> 20-30% by weight)
-   Formed in waterlogged conditions
-   Includes peatlands, bogs, fens

FAOSTAT Implementation:

-   Burned area pixels overlaid with Histosols spatial layer
-   Fires in organic soils identified independently of land cover (except excluding built-up, barren, water)
-   Different fuel consumption and emission factors applied (IPCC Wetlands Supplement 2014)

Access:

-   FAO Soils Portal: http://www.fao.org/soils-portal/
-   IIASA: https://iiasa.ac.at/models-and-data/hwsd

Critical Limitation:

-   FAOSTAT organic soil fire emissions set to zero outside Southeast Asia due to:
-   High uncertainty in peat fire detection
-   Limited validation data - Difficulty distinguishing drained vs. undrained peatlands
-   Uncertainty in human influence on fire occurrence

Supplementary file `AllFires_OrganicSoils.csv` contains global estimates for research purposes.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### IPCC Climate Zones Layer

Dataset Overview:\
IPCC Climate Zones classify global climate into categories used for stratifying biomass growth rates, decomposition rates, and fire parameters.

Classification:

-   Polar
-   Boreal (Cold)
-   Temperate (Warm temperate, Cool temperate)
-   Tropical (Tropical wet, Tropical moist, Tropical dry, Tropical montane)

Source:

-   Joint Research Centre (JRC) Climate Zones layer
-   Based on IPCC climatic zone definitions [@ipccChapter3Consistent2006]

FAOSTAT Implementation:

-   Savanna fire fuel consumption values vary by climate zone (Table 2.4)
-   Organic soil emission factors stratified by climate [@ipcc2013RevisedSupplementary2014]
-   Links IPCC methodology to geographic reality

Access:

-   JRC European Soil Data Centre (ESDAC): https://esdac.jrc.ec.europa.eu/
-   Reference: JRC (2010) Climatic Zone layer

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Google Earth Engine Setup

Platform Overview:\
Google Earth Engine is a cloud-based geospatial analysis platform providing:

-   Petabyte-scale satellite imagery archives
-   Planetary-scale computational infrastructure
-   JavaScript and Python APIs
-   Web-based Code Editor

FAOSTAT Workflow Architecture:

1.  Data Ingestion: All datasets (MCD64A1, MCD12Q1, GAUL, etc.) available as pre-processed GEE assets
2.  Pixel-Level Processing: Fire algorithm applied to all global pixels
3.  Zonal Statistics: Country-level aggregation via GAUL boundaries
4.  Export: Results exported as CSV tables for FAOSTAT database

Getting Started:

1.  Account Registration:

    -   Sign up at https://earthengine.google.com/signup/
    -   Academic/NGO accounts typically approved within 1-2 days

2.  Code Editor Access:

    -   Navigate to https://code.earthengine.google.com/
    -   JavaScript environment for interactive analysis

3.  Python API Setup (optional):

    ``` python
    pip install earthengine-api
    earthengine authenticate
    ```

4.  Key GEE Concepts:

    -   `ImageCollection`: Time series of raster images
    -   `Image`: Single raster dataset
    -   `FeatureCollection`: Vector dataset (e.g., country boundaries)
    -   `Reducer`: Aggregation functions (sum, mean, etc.)

FAOSTAT-Relevant GEE Assets:[^index-6]

``` javascript
// Burned Area
var burnedArea = ee.ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MCD64A1");

// Land Cover
var landCover = ee.ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MCD12Q1");

// Country Boundaries
var countries = ee.FeatureCollection("FAO/GAUL/2015/level0");
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[^index-1]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch03_Land%20Representation.pdf#page=35>

[^index-2]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=50>

[^index-3]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2006gl/pdf/4_Volume4/V4_04_Ch4_Forest_Land.pdf#page=27>

[^index-4]: Note to self: 2025 wishlist

[^index-5]: Direct Page Link: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=71>

[^index-6]: Code provided Python and R, with some cells in Java for use natively in Earth Engine Codespace
